beats.text:
I am an old man. I live here, in this ancient house, surrounded by huge, unkempt gardens.

The peasantry who inhabit the wilderness beyond say that I am mad. That is because I will have nothing to do with them.

I live here alone with my old sister, who is also my housekeeper.

I have one friend, a dog. Yes, I would sooner have old Pepper than the rest of Creation together. He, at least, understands me — and has sense enough to leave me alone when I am in my [pulse color=#2a2a4a]dark moods[/pulse].

I have decided to start a kind of diary; it may enable me to record some of the thoughts and feelings that I cannot express to anyone; but, beyond this, I am anxious to make some record of the strange things that I have heard and seen, during many years of loneliness, in this weird old building.

This house — how ancient it is. Little curved towers and pinnacles, with outlines suggestive of leaping flames, predominate; while the body of the building is in the form of a circle.

I have heard there is an old story, told among the country people, to the effect that the devil built the place.

For a couple of centuries, this house has had a bad reputation, and until I bought it, for more than eighty years no one had lived here.

I have ceased to deny that things happen in this old house — things I cannot explain.

It was not Halloween. If I were telling a story for amusement's sake, I should probably place it on that night of nights; but this is a true record.

It was after midnight on the morning of the twenty-first day of January. I was sitting reading, in my study. Pepper lay sleeping, near my chair.

Without warning, the flames of the two candles went low, and then shone with a ghastly [wave][color=#00aa44]green[/color][/wave] effulgence.

Then the lights sank into a dull, ruddy tint — and the room glowed with a strange, heavy, [pulse color=#8b0000]crimson[/pulse] twilight.

Down on the floor, I heard a faint, frightened whimper.

Something pressed itself in between my two feet. It was Pepper, cowering under my dressing gown.

Pepper, usually as brave as a lion.

It was this movement of the dog's, I think, that gave me the first twinge of real fear.

I did not move. I could think of nothing better to do than wait. A sort of dreamy indifference stole over me, banishing, altogether, the fear that had begun to [shake]grip[/shake] me.

Away at the far end of the room, I became conscious of a faint glow.

Steadily it grew — filling the room with gleams of quivering green light — then sank, and changed into a deep, somber [pulse color=#8b0000]crimson[/pulse] that strengthened, and lit up the room with a flood of awful glory.

The light came from the end wall, and grew ever brighter, until its intolerable glare caused my eyes acute pain.

I closed them. When I opened them again — instead of looking at the redness, I was staring [shake]through[/shake] it.

Through it, and through the wall beyond.

I was looking out onto a vast plain, lit with the same gloomy twilight that pervaded the room.

The immensity of this plain scarcely can be conceived. In no part could I perceive its confines. It seemed to broaden and spread out, so that the eye failed to perceive any limitations.

Suddenly, I became conscious that I was no longer in the chair.

I seemed to be [wave]hovering[/wave] above it — and then I was outside in the night, floating like a bubble up through the darkness. An icy coldness enfolded me, so that I shivered.

I glanced behind, and saw the earth — a small crescent of blue light, receding away to my left.

Further off, the sun — a [pulse color=#ffffaa]splash of white flame[/pulse] — burned vividly against the dark.

And I drove onward, outward, into the expanse of the unknown.

A great while seemed to pass over me. I had passed beyond the fixed stars and plunged into the huge blackness that waits beyond.

The atrocious darkness seemed to creep into my soul, and I became filled with [shake]fear[/shake] and despair.

Then, against the impalpable blackness, there grew a faint tinge of blood — extraordinarily remote and mistlike.

Slowly, the distant redness became plainer and larger, spreading out into a great, somber glare — [pulse color=#6a1a1a]dull and tremendous[/pulse].

I was descending now, sinking into a great sea of sullen, red-hued clouds.

Below me was the stupendous plain I had seen from my study — the Plain that stands upon the borders of the Silences.

I landed, and stood, surrounded by a great waste of loneliness.

The place was lit with a gloomy twilight that gave an impression of indescribable desolation. Everywhere I looked — nothing but the same flat weariness of interminable plain.

Afar to my right, within the sky, there burnt a gigantic ring of [pulse color=#8b2000]dull-red fire[/pulse], from the outer edge of which were projected huge, writhing flames, darted and jagged.

The interior of this ring was black — [shake]black[/shake] as the gloom of the outer night.

It was from this extraordinary sun that the place derived its doleful light.

Gradually, I found that I was being borne forward, floating across the flat waste.

For what seemed an eternity, I moved onward. Always, I searched for some new thing to break the monotony — but there was no change.

Only loneliness. [wave]Silence.[/wave] Desert.

At first, I saw it far ahead — like a long hillock on the surface of the Plain.

Then, as I drew nearer, I saw that I had been mistaken. Instead of a low hill, I made out a chain of great [pulse color=#3a2a3a]mountains[/pulse], whose distant peaks towered up into the red gloom — until they were almost lost to sight.
